message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Verdict.label is unvalidated; a non-enum label crashes scorecard aggregation with an opaque KeyError
DIMENSION: public API & adapter-Protocol stability
SEVERITY: HIGH
FILE: src/assay_engine/methodology/verdict.py:31-40; src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:83-100 
DESCRIPTION: Verdict.__post_init__ freezes evidence but never checks that `label` is a VerdictLabel. A study-supplied ClaimConfirmer/HeldOutConfirmer (the public Protocols in adjudication.py / discovery.py) returns a Verdict; an adapter that builds one with a bare string label (label='supported') — or via dataclasses.replace — produces a structurally-valid frozen Verdict. _score then does `counts[v.label] += 1` against a dict keyed only by the three VerdictLabel members, raising KeyError('supported') instead of a contract error. PoC verified: Verdict('h1', label='supported', 'rule') feeds _score → KeyError('supported'). In run_study this surfaces deep in the ADJUDICATE/SCORE phase as an opaque KeyError rather than a typed FirewallViolation/ValueError naming the offending hypothesis, and the pipeline's verdict.label.value provenance lines (pipeline.py:438,488) would AttributeError on a string. The contract gives adapter authors no runtime feedback that they returned the wrong type.
SUGGESTED FIX: Add `if not isinstance(self.label, VerdictLabel): raise TypeError(...)` to Verdict.__post_init__ (and similarly validate hypothesis_id is a non-empty str). This converts a misimplemented confirmer into an immediate, attributable contract error at Verdict construction rather than an opaque KeyError during scoring.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim by reading the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""The three verdicts (ADR-0004, METHODOLOGY.md §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test returns exactly one of supported / contradicted / indeterminate — never
4	a forced binary. Every verdict carries the evidence and the decision rule behind it, so a
5	reviewer can re-derive it.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
11	from enum import Enum
12	from typing import Any, Mapping
13	
14	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
15	
16	
17	class VerdictLabel(Enum):
18	    SUPPORTED = "supported"
19	    CONTRADICTED = "contradicted"
20	    INDETERMINATE = "indeterminate"
21	
22	
23	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
24	class Verdict:
25	    """The outcome of one confirmatory test, structured identically for all three labels.
26	
27	    ``indeterminate`` is first-class: underpowered, beyond the measurement's reach, or a
28	    disagreement plausibly attributable to a method limitation rather than a claim error.
29	    """
30	
31	    hypothesis_id: str
32	    label: VerdictLabel
33	    decision_rule: str
34	    statistic: float | None = None
35	    threshold: float | None = None
36	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	    notes: str = ""
38	
39	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
40	        object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", freeze_mapping(self.evidence))
41	
42	    @property
43	    def is_indeterminate(self) -> bool:
44	        return self.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
45	
46	    @classmethod
47	    def supported(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
48	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, decision_rule, **kw)
49	
50	    @classmethod
51	    def contradicted(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
52	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED, decision_rule, **kw)
53	
54	    @classmethod
55	    def indeterminate(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
56	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE, decision_rule, **kw)
57

1	"""Adjudication runner — the engine owns the firewall guarantee, not each study (ADR-0008).
2	
3	METHODOLOGY.md describes the adjudication pipeline: build a baseline *blind* to the external
4	claims (Firewall A), convert each claim into a typed, pre-stated hypothesis, test each against
5	the blind baseline, and *score the external source* against the validated baseline (§5). Until
6	now the engine shipped the parts (a custodial :class:`ClaimBlindGuard`, the confirmatory tests,
7	the verdicts) but no composition — so Firewall A held only if a study wired it correctly by
8	hand. For a blueprint inherited by many studies, that pushes the single most important
9	methodological guarantee onto every cloner, where it is easy to get silently wrong (build the
10	baseline with the claims in scope and nothing stops you).
11	
12	:func:`adjudicate` makes the guarantee structural: it builds the baseline *inside* a sealed
13	claim-guard that holds the claims source, so the baseline builder cannot reach the claims
14	during construction (any attempt raises ``FirewallViolation``); only after the baseline is
15	built does the runner release the claims and adjudicate. It then aggregates the verdicts into a
16	:class:`SourceScorecard` — the §5 "score the source" step, expressed as alignment *frequency*
17	on decisive claims, explicitly NOT a normative judgement of the source's quality (richer
18	dimensions — where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps — are data-surfaced downstream, per §5).
19	"""
20	
21	from __future__ import annotations
22	
23	import datetime as _dt
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Callable, Iterable, Protocol
26	
27	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord, ExternalClaimsSource
28	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
31	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
32	    TimestampAuthority,
33	    require_preregistered,
34	)
35	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
36	
37	if TYPE_CHECKING:
38	    # Annotations only — importing baseline at runtime would invert the layering
39	    # (baseline.toolkit depends on methodology.firewalls) and create a cycle.
40	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact, BaselineBuilder
41	
42	
43	class ClaimConfirmer(Protocol):
44	    """Study-supplied: confirm one claim-derived hypothesis against the (blind) baseline.
45	
46	    The study owns this because only it knows the baseline's structure and how a given claim
47	    maps onto a measurement of it; the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives
48	    (``confirm_whole_corpus`` / ``confirm_unit_level``) the study uses inside it.
49	    """
50	
51	    def __call__(
52	        self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord
53	    ) -> Verdict: ...
54	
55	
56	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
57	class SourceScorecard:
58	    """How an external source aligns with the independent baseline (METHODOLOGY.md §5).
59	
60	    ``alignment_rate`` is supported / (supported + contradicted) — the fraction of *decisive*
61	    claims where the blind baseline agrees with the source's assertion. ``indeterminate`` is
62	    excluded from the denominator (the method could not decide; counting it would understate
63	    or overstate alignment). This is a frequency, not a verdict on whether the source is
64	    "good": it says how often an independent reading of the data corroborates the source.
65	    """
66	
67	    source: str
68	    n_supported: int
69	    n_contradicted: int
70	    n_indeterminate: int
71	    alignment_rate: float | None
72	    verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
73	
74	    @property
75	    def total(self) -> int:
76	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted + self.n_indeterminate
77	
78	    @property
79	    def decisive(self) -> int:
80	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted
81	
82	
83	def _score(source: str, verdicts: list[Verdict]) -> SourceScorecard:
84	    counts = {
85	        VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 0,
86	        VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED: 0,
87	        VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE: 0,
88	    }
89	    for v in verdicts:
90	        counts[v.label] += 1
91	    decisive = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] + counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED]
92	    rate = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] / decisive if decisive else None
93	    return SourceScorecard(
94	        source=source,
95	        n_supported=counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED],
96	        n_contradicted=counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED],
97	        n_indeterminate=counts[VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE],
98	        alignment_rate=rate,
99	        verdicts=tuple(verdicts),
100	    )
101	
102	
103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived
130	       hypothesis must therefore be locked *before* ``adjudicate`` is called — ``hypothesis_for``
131	       looks up an already-locked hypothesis; locking on demand inside it (after the baseline
132	       exists) is rejected, so the hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline.
133	    3. The verdict's ``hypothesis_id`` must match the hypothesis it answers (no misattribution),
134	       and the verdicts are aggregated into a :class:`SourceScorecard`.
135	
136	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
137	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
138	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default, so a study must supply a real one.
139	
140	    Returns ``(baseline, scorecard)``. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl.
141	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.PreRegistrationError`) on any
142	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity mismatch, a non-``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` (data-surfaced)
143	    hypothesis, or a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered before confirmation.
144	    """
145	    # The claims source stays in THIS function's local scope and is never given to the builder.
146	    # The builder receives a guard holding nothing (a blind-mode signal only), so there is no
147	    # object reachable from the builder that contains the claims — not even via the guard's
148	    # internals. This is stronger than a custodial guard, whose private `_claims_source` a
149	    # determined builder could read past the sealed `release()` check (adversarial review).
150	    # Captured BEFORE the baseline is built: an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis derives from the
151	    # external claim, not from the baseline, so it must be pre-registered before the baseline (the
152	    # answer) exists. Using this as `not_after` makes "lock before the result is in hand" real —
153	    # a hypothesis locked on demand inside `hypothesis_for` (i.e. after the build) is rejected,
154	    # forcing studies to pre-lock their claim-derived hypotheses (#83).
155	    not_after = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	
157	    guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource] = ClaimBlindGuard()
158	    with guard.sealed():
159	        baseline = baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
160	
161	    scorecard = adjudicate_with_baseline(
162	        baseline,
163	        claims_source.claims(),
164	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for,
165	        confirm=confirm,
166	        authority=authority,
167	        not_after=not_after,
168	        source_name=source_name,
169	    )
170	    return baseline, scorecard
171	
172	
173	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
174	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
175	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
176	    *,
177	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
178	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
179	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
180	    not_after: _dt.datetime,
181	    source_name: str = "external",
182	    on_step: "Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None" = None,
183	) -> SourceScorecard:
184	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
185	
186	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
187	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
188	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and
189	    for passing the ``not_after`` ordering instant captured *before* that build — so the
190	    lock-before-baseline guarantee is preserved across the shared-baseline composition.
191	
192	    ``claims`` is **materialized once** here (not re-pulled from a source): a caller that gated
193	    or recorded a claim snapshot must adjudicate *that* snapshot, not a set a re-iterable source
194	    could change between the gate and the scoring (adversarial review #95). Enforces the same
195	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity + pre-registration checks as :func:`adjudicate`.
196	    """
197	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
198	    for claim in list(claims):  # claims used only after the blind baseline is built
199	        hypothesis = hypothesis_for(claim)
200	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
201	            raise FirewallViolation(
202	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
203	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
204	            )
205	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
206	            raise FirewallViolation(
207	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
208	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
209	            )
210	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
211	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
212	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
213	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
214	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
215	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
216	            raise FirewallViolation(
217	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
218	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
219	            )
220	        verdicts.append(verdict)
221	        if on_step is not None:
222	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
223	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
224	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
225	
226	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)
227

content.text: Now let me verify the PoC and check how the verdict identity check works. Note line 215 already checks `verdict.hypothesis_id` — but a string label would still pass that. Let me check pipeline.py provenance lines and run the PoC.

content.content:
55	    :mod:`discovery`) enforce it via a supplied ``TimestampAuthority``. Use
56	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.lock_hypothesis` to produce a hypothesis
57	    whose lock actually verifies; a hand-set ``timestamp_proof`` string satisfies ``locked``
58	    but will fail ``verify_preregistration``.
59	    """
60	
61	    hypothesis_id: str
62	    statement: str
63	    kind: HypothesisKind
64	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
65	    test_name: str
66	    decision_rule: str
67	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
68	    locked_at: str | None = None
69	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
70	    # The predicted direction, fixed at pre-registration so it cannot be chosen post-hoc
71	    # after seeing the baseline (audit pass 2, issue #24). For an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis
72	    # the adapter derives this from the claim's assertion, not from the baseline.
73	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None
74	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
75	
76	    @property
77	    def locked(self) -> bool:
78	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
79	
80	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
81	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
82	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
83	        object.__setattr__(self, "params", freeze_mapping(self.params))
84

110	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
111	    if not held_out.units:
112	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
113	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
114	        raise FirewallViolation(
115	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
116	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
117	        )
118	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
119	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
120	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
121	            raise FirewallViolation(
122	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
123	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
124	            )
125	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
126	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
127	        require_preregistered(
128	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
129	        )
130	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
131	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
132	            raise FirewallViolation(
133	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
134	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
135	            )
136	        verdicts.append(verdict)
137	    return verdicts
138

content.text:
I have full confirmation. The assessment is accurate on all mechanics.

Assessment of severity. The bug is real and reproducible, but I should calibrate honestly:

- The trigger requires a study/adapter author to misimplement the `ClaimConfirmer`/`HeldOutConfirmer` Protocol by returning `Verdict(..., label='supported', ...)` with a raw string instead of `VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED`. This is a programming error by the adapter author, not attacker-controllable or data-driven.
- The provided factory methods (`Verdict.supported/contradicted/indeterminate`) make correct construction natural, but the public dataclass constructor accepts any value, and the type annotation `label: VerdictLabel` is not runtime-enforced.
- Consequence is exactly an opaque `KeyError('supported')` / `AttributeError("'str' object has no attribute 'value'")` deep in CONFIRM/SCORE, with no mention of the offending hypothesis — versus the engine's clear pattern elsewhere (FirewallViolation/ValueError name the offending claim/hypothesis). This contradicts the engine's stated contract-feedback philosophy ("the engine owns the firewall guarantee," fail-loud-with-attribution everywhere else).
- The adjudication runner already validates `hypothesis_id` identity (line 215) and origin, but does NOT validate the verdict's `label` type — a genuine gap the assessor correctly identified.

This is a real defect but it is HIGH-leaning-MEDIUM: it's a contract/DX/error-quality defect on the public adapter Protocol boundary, not a correctness bug in normal operation, not a crash on valid input, not security/data-integrity. It degrades attributability of an adapter error. I'll rate MEDIUM — it's a legitimate production-readiness gap (poor error attribution on the documented extension boundary) but its severity is bounded by requiring an adapter-author mistake to trigger, and the failure is a loud crash (not silent wrong results). HIGH would overstate it since HIGH is typically reserved for silent corruption, security, or crashes on valid input.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real production-readiness defect, with one severity recalibration (HIGH -> MEDIUM).

Verified mechanics (PoC run in the project venv):
- `Verdict.__post_init__` (verdict.py:39-40) only freezes `evidence`; it does NOT type-check `label`. `Verdict('h1', label='supported', decision_rule='rule')` constructs a structurally valid, frozen Verdict whose `label` is a bare `str` (PoC: "Constructed OK ... label type: <class 'str'>").
- `_score` (adjudication.py:83-100) keys `counts` by the three `VerdictLabel` members and does `counts[v.label] += 1` (line 90). PoC: feeding the string-label verdict raises `KeyError('supported')` — an opaque error that names neither the contract nor the offending hypothesis.
- The pipeline's provenance lines access `verdict.label.value` (pipeline.py:438, 440, 445, 486, 488). PoC: `v.label.value` raises `AttributeError("'str' object has no attribute 'value'")`. So in DISCOVERY/CONFIRM the AttributeError at 438 surfaces even before any scoring; in ADJUDICATE the same AttributeError (486/488) and/or the _score KeyError surface.

The defect is genuine and contradicts the engine's own design philosophy: every other identity/contract violation on the adapter boundary (adjudication.py:200-219, discovery.py:120-135) raises a typed FirewallViolation/ValueError that names the offending claim/hypothesis; Hypothesis.__post_init__ (hypothesis.py:80-82) validates a semantic invariant. The Verdict label type is the one unvalidated field on the documented public Protocol return type (ClaimConfirmer adjudication.py:43-53, HeldOutConfirmer discovery.py:42-45), giving a misimplementing adapter author an unattributable KeyError/AttributeError instead of an immediate, named contract error.

Severity recalibration to MEDIUM (assessor said HIGH): the trigger requires an adapter author to misconstruct a Verdict (pass a raw string instead of VerdictLabel, e.g. not using the provided Verdict.supported/.contradicted/.indeterminate factories). It is not attacker- or data-controllable, and the failure mode is a LOUD crash, not silent wrong results or data corruption. The impact is degraded error attribution / developer experience on the extension seam — a legitimate production-readiness gap for a "blueprint inherited by many studies," but bounded below HIGH (which I reserve for silent corruption, security, or crashes on valid input). The suggested fix (isinstance check in __post_init__ raising TypeError, naming hypothesis_id) is correct and idiomatic for this codebase; validating hypothesis_id is a non-empty str is a reasonable extension but lower value since adjudication already checks hypothesis_id identity at line 215.

Not a pass-1 re-report and not a documented out-of-scope constraint (none of the hard constraints — loopback, OAuth, ADR-0002/0006 — touch verdict label validation).

  Verdict('h1', label='supported', decision_rule='rule')  -> constructs OK, label type is <class 'str'>
  _score('ext', [that verdict])  -> KeyError('supported')
  that_verdict.label.value        -> AttributeError("'str' object has no attribute 'value'")

Code:
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/verdict.py:31-40 — `label: VerdictLabel` annotated but __post_init__ (lines 39-40) only freezes evidence, no isinstance check.
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:83-100 — _score keys counts by VerdictLabel only; line 90 `counts[v.label] += 1` raises KeyError on a string label. (Note: line 215 already validates verdict.hypothesis_id but not label type.)
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:438,440,445,486,488 — access verdict.label.value, AttributeError on a string label.
- Contrast (engine's fail-loud-with-attribution norm): adjudication.py:200-219, discovery.py:120-135 (typed FirewallViolation naming the claim/hypothesis); hypothesis.py:80-82 (__post_init__ validates a semantic invariant).

content.text:
Verification complete. The finding is REAL but I recalibrated severity from HIGH to MEDIUM.

Summary:
- Confirmed via PoC in the project venv: `Verdict('h1', label='supported', decision_rule='rule')` constructs a valid frozen Verdict with a raw `str` label because `Verdict.__post_init__` (verdict.py:39-40) only freezes evidence and never type-checks `label`.
- `_score` (adjudication.py:90) raises `KeyError('supported')`, and the pipeline provenance lines (pipeline.py:438, 440, 445, 486, 488) raise `AttributeError("'str' object has no attribute 'value'")` — both opaque, neither naming the offending hypothesis.
- This contradicts the engine's own fail-loud-with-attribution norm everywhere else on the adapter boundary (typed FirewallViolation/ValueError naming the claim/hypothesis; hypothesis.py:80-82 validates a semantic invariant).

Why MEDIUM not HIGH: the trigger requires an adapter author to misimplement the public Confirmer Protocol (pass a string rather than using the provided `Verdict.supported/.contradicted/.indeterminate` factories). It is not data- or attacker-controllable, and the failure is a loud crash, not silent wrong results or corruption. It is a genuine contract/error-attribution gap on the documented extension seam — worth fixing for a blueprint inherited by many studies — but bounded below HIGH. The assessor's suggested fix (isinstance check raising TypeError in `__post_init__`) is correct and idiomatic.
